explanation: First, we identify that 'once the start-up has been completed successfully' is captured by the atomic proposition startUpDone. In LTL, 'once ... has been completed successfully' often means: in any future where this event happens, a certain property must hold from that time on ('after startUpDone'). 'The user must select “New Patient” or “Resume Ventilation” before the system transitions to self-test mode' means that between the startUpDone event and the SelfTestMode event, at least once, either newPatient or resumeVentilation must be true. So, whenever we get startUpDone, before we reach SelfTestMode, it should happen that (newPatient or resumeVentilation) holds. In LTL, this is best modeled as: Globally, if startUpDone occurs, then (until SelfTestMode, (newPatient or resumeVentilation) must occur). This is classically written as: G(startUpDone -> ((!(SelfTestMode)) U ((newPatient | resumeVentilation) & (!(SelfTestMode) U SelfTestMode)))). But simpler and equivalent: G(startUpDone -> F((newPatient | resumeVentilation) & ( (newPatient | resumeVentilation) U SelfTestMode))). In practice, we want to say that after startUpDone, (newPatient or resumeVentilation) must be seen before SelfTestMode. The standard idiom in LTL for 'p must occur before q, once r' is G(r -> (!q U (p & X(!q U q)))) if p must occur strictly before q, or just G(r -> (¬q U (p & (¬q U q)))) if p can coincide with q. For clarity, use: G(startUpDone -> ((!SelfTestMode) U ( (newPatient | resumeVentilation) & (!SelfTestMode U SelfTestMode) )))

the user must select “New Patient” or “Resume Ventilation” before the system transitions to self-test mode

explanation:
The sentence states: 'Once the start-up has been completed successfully the user must select “New Patient” or “Resume Ventilation” before the system transitions to self-test mode.' 

1. 'Once the start-up has been completed successfully' can be mapped to the event 'startUpDone' becoming true.
2. 'the user must select “New Patient” or “Resume Ventilation” before the system transitions to self-test mode' intends a temporal ordering: after 'startUpDone', before 'SelfTestMode', either 'newPatient' or 'resumeVentilation' must occur.

We express this by saying: Globally, Whenever (startUpDone occurs), then (between now and the next SelfTestMode, at some point, newPatient or resumeVentilation must hold). 

This is a 'leads to before' pattern, which can be formalized: G( startUpDone -> ((!(SelfTestMode)) U ((newPatient | resumeVentilation) & (!(SelfTestMode)) U SelfTestMode)) )

But more precisely, for every time startUpDone happens, it is required that (newPatient or resumeVentilation) happens before SelfTestMode. In LTL, this is: G( startUpDone -> ( (!SelfTestMode) U ((newPatient | resumeVentilation) & (!SelfTestMode) U SelfTestMode) ) )
However, the simpler way to enforce this is: G( (startUpDone & F SelfTestMode) -> F( (newPatient | resumeVentilation) & X(F SelfTestMode) ) )
But such patterns are complicated, so typically, we use: G( (startUpDone & (!SelfTestMode) ) -> ( (!SelfTestMode) U ( (newPatient | resumeVentilation) ) ) ), and additionally, that SelfTestMode should not occur before newPatient or resumeVentilation.

This can be compactly expressed as: G( (startUpDone & F(SelfTestMode)) -> ( (!SelfTestMode) U (newPatient | resumeVentilation) ) )

This says: globally, if startUpDone has happened and SelfTestMode will occur in the future, then before SelfTestMode, newPatient or resumeVentilation must occur.

explanation: The sentence expresses that once start-up ('startUpDone') has been completed, the user must (before entering SelfTestMode) select either 'newPatient' or 'resumeVentilation'. This is a classic 'leads-to' pattern with a constraint between events. We must capture: 1) Once 'startUpDone' becomes true, it must remain so (or at least, until SelfTestMode, we only care that it has happened), 2) Before 'SelfTestMode' becomes true after 'startUpDone', at least once either 'newPatient' or 'resumeVentilation' must be true. The most direct LTL formalization is to say: "globally, whenever startUpDone first becomes true, and before the next SelfTestMode, at some point either newPatient or resumeVentilation is true." This translates as: G((startUpDone & (!SelfTestMode U (newPatient | resumeVentilation | SelfTestMode))) -> F((newPatient | resumeVentilation) & (!SelfTestMode) ) before the next SelfTestMode). Or, more clearly: G((startUpDone & !SelfTestMode) -> ( (!SelfTestMode) U ((newPatient | resumeVentilation) & !SelfTestMode) ) ). It can also be captured by: G( (startUpDone & !SelfTestMode) -> F( (!SelfTestMode) & (newPatient | resumeVentilation) ) ), guaranteeing that after startUpDone (and while still not in SelfTestMode), eventually (before SelfTestMode), either newPatient or resumeVentilation will occur. This captures the intended causal sequence.

explanation: The sentence describes a temporal behavior: after the start-up is completed successfully (represented by startUpDone), it is required that before the system transitions to self-test mode (represented by SelfTestMode), the user must select either 'New Patient' (newPatient) or 'Resume Ventilation' (resumeVentilation). In LTL, 'once A then B before C' is translated by ensuring that after A occurs, before C, B must happen. The approach is to globally say: If at the current moment, startUpDone holds, then from now on, until SelfTestMode occurs, either newPatient or resumeVentilation must have occurred before SelfTestMode happens. To ensure one of these actions is selected before transitioning to SelfTestMode, we say that startUpDone implies: it is not the case that SelfTestMode occurs before either newPatient or resumeVentilation. More precisely: G((startUpDone & !SelfTestMode & !(newPatient | resumeVentilation)) -> (!(SelfTestMode) U (newPatient | resumeVentilation))). Alternatively, after startUpDone holds, F(newPatient | resumeVentilation) must be true before any SelfTestMode. Thus, the clear and canonical LTL for this requirement is: G((startUpDone & F SelfTestMode) -> F((newPatient | resumeVentilation) & X(F SelfTestMode))). This says: Whenever startup is done and SelfTestMode will eventually be entered, then eventually a newPatient or resumeVentilation selection occurs and afterwards (in the future) SelfTestMode occurs.

explanation:
The sentence states: 'Once the start-up has been completed successfully the user must select “New Patient” or “Resume Ventilation” before the system transitions to self-test mode.' This can be mapped to atomic propositions:
- startUpDone: start-up complete
- newPatient: 'New Patient' is selected
- resumeVentilation: 'Resume Ventilation' is selected
- SelfTestMode: system transitions to self-test mode

'Once the start-up has been completed' refers to the point in time after startUpDone becomes true. After this point, before the system transitions to SelfTestMode, the user must select newPatient or resumeVentilation. In LTL, this is most robustly captured as: it is not possible, after startUpDone, for SelfTestMode to occur without having newPatient or resumeVentilation selected since startUpDone. That is, globally, if startUpDone has occurred, then between startUpDone and the next SelfTestMode, whenever SelfTestMode occurs, it must be the case that (newPatient OR resumeVentilation) was selected at some point after startUpDone and before SelfTestMode (or at the same time as SelfTestMode). This is classically expressed as:

G(startUpDone -> ( !(SelfTestMode) U ( (newPatient | resumeVentilation) & X( (!(SelfTestMode) U SelfTestMode) )) ))

But a more readable, standard variant is:

G( (startUpDone & F SelfTestMode) -> ( ( ( !(SelfTestMode) U (newPatient | resumeVentilation) ) & F(SelfTestMode) ) ) )

But more simply, the requirement is: After startUpDone, the first SelfTestMode occurrence must be preceded by (newPatient | resumeVentilation).

A succinct and correct LTL is:

G( startUpDone -> ( (!(SelfTestMode) U (newPatient | resumeVentilation)) U SelfTestMode ) )

This means: After startUpDone, before SelfTestMode, 'newPatient' or 'resumeVentilation' must be chosen; when SelfTestMode occurs, at least one of those has occurred since startUpDone, and SelfTestMode can only happen after at least one such action.

Step-by-step mapping is given below.

explanation:
The sentence says: Whenever 'the start-up has been completed successfully' (which maps to atomic proposition startUpDone), then at some point before (that is, strictly before) the system transitions to SelfTestMode, the user must select either 'New Patient' (newPatient) or 'Resume Ventilation' (resumeVentilation). In LTL, this means after startUpDone becomes true, before SelfTestMode becomes true, one of newPatient or resumeVentilation must be true at least once.

This can be formalized as: Globally, if startUpDone occurs, then (before the next occurrence of SelfTestMode) it is required that either newPatient or resumeVentilation has happened.

We express this by using the 'until' (U) operator: after startUpDone, it must be the case that (¬SelfTestMode) holds until (newPatient ∨ resumeVentilation). Moreover, (newPatient ∨ resumeVentilation) should happen before SelfTestMode becomes true.

To encode 'one of newPatient or resumeVentilation before SelfTestMode', we can formally say:
Globally: whenever startUpDone occurs, then it must be the case that (¬SelfTestMode) U (newPatient ∨ resumeVentilation), and afterwards (i.e., after the selection) the next thing could be SelfTestMode.

But, to closely follow the intended meaning, what we need is:
Globally, whenever startUpDone becomes true, then (newPatient ∨ resumeVentilation) must occur before SelfTestMode.
This can be written as:
G (startUpDone -> (¬SelfTestMode U (newPatient ∨ resumeVentilation)))
This says: from a point where startUpDone happens, it must be the case that before SelfTestMode, at least one of (newPatient ∨ resumeVentilation) happens.

explanation: The natural language sentence indicates that after the system finishes the start-up procedure successfully (startUpDone), before transitioning to SelfTestMode, the user must select either 'New Patient' (newPatient) or 'Resume Ventilation' (resumeVentilation). This describes a temporal constraint: after startUpDone, and before SelfTestMode, one of the two selections must occur. In LTL, the most direct way is to say that once startUpDone occurs, we require that (before SelfTestMode happens), newPatient or resumeVentilation must occur. This is classically specified by the 'leadsto' property: whenever startUpDone occurs, it must be followed by (before SelfTestMode) at least one occurrence of newPatient or resumeVentilation. More formally, after startUpDone, we insist that (¬SelfTestMode) holds until (newPatient | resumeVentilation), and that afterwards, SelfTestMode may happen. So, globally: if startUpDone occurs, then until (newPatient | resumeVentilation), SelfTestMode does not happen. This is encoded as: G( (startUpDone & (¬(newPatient | resumeVentilation)) ) -> (¬SelfTestMode U (newPatient | resumeVentilation)) ).

explanation:
The natural language sentence describes a requirement: After the start-up has been completed (startUpDone is true), before the transition to self-test mode (SelfTestMode), the user must select either 'New Patient' (newPatient) or 'Resume Ventilation' (resumeVentilation).

Step-by-step reasoning:
1. "Once the start-up has been completed successfully": event startUpDone becomes true.
2. "before the system transitions to self-test mode": the selection (either newPatient or resumeVentilation) must occur before SelfTestMode is true. We are interested in all moments after 'startUpDone' and before 'SelfTestMode'.
3. "the user must select...": this means (newPatient or resumeVentilation) must become true before SelfTestMode.

This is a standard 'response' pattern: when 'startUpDone' occurs, (newPatient | resumeVentilation) must occur before SelfTestMode. If SelfTestMode never happens after startUpDone, then there is no violation. 

In LTL, this can be captured as:
Globally, if startUpDone becomes true, then, from that point on, until SelfTestMode, (newPatient | resumeVentilation) must hold at least once (before SelfTestMode).

This is typically encoded as:
G( (startUpDone & !(SelfTestMode)) -> ( (!SelfTestMode) U ((newPatient | resumeVentilation) & (!SelfTestMode) ) ) )

Alternatively, more succinctly: After startUpDone, before SelfTestMode, (newPatient | resumeVentilation) holds at least once before SelfTestMode:
G( (startUpDone & !SelfTestMode) -> F( (newPatient | resumeVentilation) & (!SelfTestMode) ) )
This says: Whenever startUpDone holds and SelfTestMode is not yet true, eventually (before SelfTestMode) newPatient or resumeVentilation must occur.

Best pattern is: After startUpDone and before SelfTestMode, (newPatient | resumeVentilation) holds before SelfTestMode, so:
G( startUpDone -> ( (!SelfTestMode) U ( (newPatient | resumeVentilation) & (!SelfTestMode) ) ) )

Or using response pattern (Whenever startUpDone, (newPatient | resumeVentilation) must eventually be true before any SelfTestMode):
G( startUpDone -> ( (!SelfTestMode) U (newPatient | resumeVentilation) ) )

But to be strict: After startUpDone, before SelfTestMode, eventually (newPatient | resumeVentilation) must be true before SelfTestMode.

So, the formula is:
G( startUpDone -> ( ( !SelfTestMode ) U ( ( newPatient | resumeVentilation ) ) ) )

explanation:
First, identify the relevant atomic propositions. 'start-up has been completed successfully' maps to startUpDone. 'Before the system transitions to self-test mode' maps to SelfTestMode. 'the user must select “New Patient” or “Resume Ventilation”' maps to (newPatient | resumeVentilation). The requirement is that after 'startUpDone' occurs, before SelfTestMode holds, newPatient or resumeVentilation must be selected. This is best captured by an 'until' property: after startUpDone holds, in all paths to SelfTestMode, (newPatient | resumeVentilation) must hold at least once before (and possibly at the same time as) SelfTestMode. Thus, whenever startUpDone occurs, it must be the case that until SelfTestMode, (newPatient | resumeVentilation) holds at some point. 

Formally: Globally, if startUpDone holds, then (not SelfTestMode) holds until ((newPatient | resumeVentilation) & (not SelfTestMode)), and after that, before entering SelfTestMode, one of those buttons must have been pressed. Alternatively, globally: if we ever enter SelfTestMode after startUpDone, then (newPatient | resumeVentilation) must have happened before SelfTestMode. This can be written as G((startUpDone & F SelfTestMode) -> F((newPatient | resumeVentilation) & X SelfTestMode)), or, more directly and commonly:

G((startUpDone & X (not SelfTestMode U ((newPatient | resumeVentilation) & (not SelfTestMode)))) -> F(((newPatient | resumeVentilation) & X SelfTestMode)))

But the standard way for such requirements is:
G((startUpDone & F SelfTestMode) -> F((newPatient | resumeVentilation) & X SelfTestMode))

We can interpret the requirement as: Once startUpDone holds, on every path to SelfTestMode, (newPatient | resumeVentilation) must be pressed before SelfTestMode. Thus, globally: If startUpDone is true, and eventually SelfTestMode happens, then before SelfTestMode, (newPatient | resumeVentilation) must have happened.
Final formula: G(startUpDone -> ((not SelfTestMode) U ((newPatient | resumeVentilation) & X SelfTestMode)))

explanation:
The requirement states: "Once the start-up has been completed successfully the user must select ‘New Patient’ or ‘Resume Ventilation’ before the system transitions to self-test mode." 

1. Identify atomic propositions:
- "start-up has been completed successfully" → startUpDone
- "user must select ‘New Patient’" → newPatient
- "Resume Ventilation selected" → resumeVentilation
- "system transitions to self-test mode" → SelfTestMode

2. Temporal ordering:
- "Once the start-up has been completed successfully" means that after startUpDone becomes true, a temporal requirement is imposed on all future behavior.
- The main requirement: "[must select newPatient or resumeVentilation] BEFORE [SelfTestMode]". This is a strict 'precedence' constraint: before SelfTestMode can become true (after startUpDone), at least one of newPatient or resumeVentilation must become true.

3. We can express this as: For any point, if startUpDone holds, then it is always the case (globally) that until (U) SelfTestMode happens, (!SelfTestMode), we must have not SelfTestMode unless (before) (newPatient OR resumeVentilation) happens.

In classical LTL, this is expressed as:

G(startUpDone -> ((!SelfTestMode) U ((newPatient | resumeVentilation) & (!SelfTestMode) U SelfTestMode)))

But the normalized version (and more idiomatic) is: Whenever SelfTestMode occurs after startUpDone, it must be the case that before that, newPatient or resumeVentilation occurred. This is a typical 'precondition' property and is best expressed as:

G(((startUpDone & X(F SelfTestMode)) -> (¬SelfTestMode U ((newPatient | resumeVentilation) & (¬SelfTestMode U SelfTestMode)))))

But more simply (and more standard in LTL):

For all times, if we see a transition to SelfTestMode (i.e., SelfTestMode becomes true) and startUpDone has already occurred, then previously (in LTL, this is not directly expressible, so we use the 'until' operator to model that between startUpDone and SelfTestMode, there was an occurrence of newPatient or resumeVentilation):

G( (startUpDone & (¬SelfTestMode U (SelfTestMode & F (newPatient | resumeVentilation)))) -> (F ( (newPatient | resumeVentilation) & X SelfTestMode)))

But for simple LTL, we write:

G( (startUpDone & F SelfTestMode) -> F ( (newPatient | resumeVentilation) & X SelfTestMode))

But the most precise way, going step by step:

- After startUpDone, before SelfTestMode holds, (newPatient | resumeVentilation) must occur.
- This is ‘startUpDone -> (!SelfTestMode U ((newPatient | resumeVentilation) & (!SelfTestMode U SelfTestMode)))’
- But, to ensure (newPatient | resumeVentilation) occurs *before* SelfTestMode (i.e., no path from startUpDone to SelfTestMode without passing through (newPatient | resumeVentilation)).

So, the canonical LTL is:
G(startUpDone -> ((!SelfTestMode) U ((newPatient | resumeVentilation) & X(SelfTestMode))))

If there could be *arbitrary* time between startUpDone and SelfTestMode, but the first time SelfTestMode holds, (newPatient | resumeVentilation) must have held just before (i.e., immediately before SelfTestMode). 

If it is enough that at some point after startUpDone and before SelfTestMode, (newPatient | resumeVentilation) holds, and then eventually SelfTestMode comes:
G(startUpDone -> ( (!SelfTestMode U (newPatient | resumeVentilation)) & (F SelfTestMode -> F (newPatient | resumeVentilation)) ))

But typically, the simplest formalization is:
G( (startUpDone & X(F SelfTestMode)) -> ((newPatient | resumeVentilation) U SelfTestMode) )

This says: For any moment, if startUpDone has occurred and SelfTestMode will eventually happen, then (newPatient | resumeVentilation) must occur before SelfTestMode does.

In summary, the most suitable LTL is:
G ( (startUpDone & F SelfTestMode) -> ( ( !SelfTestMode ) U ( ( newPatient | resumeVentilation ) ) ) )
